coredata.dc:title: Enhanced photoreactivity of amine-functionalized carbon nanotubes under sunlight in the aquatic environment
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Chiaying
Huang, Yu
coredata.dc:description: To overcome the hydrophobic nature of pristine carbonaceous materials such as carbon nanotubes (CNTs) and to make them available for intended applications, chemically covalent functionalization tailoring these materials is widely applied. However, the addition of surface functional moieties often changes the fundamental properties of the parent materials and introduces great variations that hinder a full understanding of and unified conclusions about their environmental implications. In this work, we studied the photoactivity of covalently functionalized CNTs in the aquatic environment under sunlight irradiation. The results indicate an enhanced photoreactivity of CNTs with amine functional groups resulting from a greater excited triplet state formation and a restored electronic structure after the secondary functionalization. Photogenerated singlet oxygen was produced directly through a photosensitization process in which the photoexcited CNTs transferred energy to oxygen, as well as produced indirectly from the aqueous reactions of superoxide radical. The superior photoreactive behaviors of engineered nanomaterials with amine functionalization in terms of reactive oxygen species generation in aquatic environments not only raise ecological concerns, but also render these functionalized engineered nanomaterials useful as water treatment agents against pollutants or microorganisms that can be destroyed by singlet oxygen or hydroxyl radicals.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Emerging carbonaceous nanomaterials
Reactive oxygen species
Photosensitization
Fate
Singlet oxygen

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determining the formation rates of triplet states
Excited triplet state formation of CNTs under sunlight irradiation

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Ministry of Science and Technology (MOST) of Taiwan
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Science and Technology, Taiwan

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge financial support from the Ministry of Science and Technology (MOST) of Taiwan under grant number MOST 105-2221-E-005-003-MY3 . Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematics of the reactions for CNT functionalization.
TGA and DSC of N-SWCNTs and C-SWCNTs.
Raman spectra of N-SWCNTs and C-SWCNTs.
Electrophoretic mobility (EPM) as a function of pH. Data points represent averages of triplicate analyses, where the error bars represent one standard deviation.
XPS N1s spectra of N-SWCNTs and C-SWCNTs.
cis, trans-HDA isomer formation as a function of exposure time to simulated sunlight in 6 mg L−1 CNT suspensions and control solutions (in the absence of CNTs).

Loss of the singlet oxygen acceptor, furfuryl alcohol (FFA), under simulated sunlight exposure in 6 mg L−1 CNT suspensions at pH = 7, 25°C of (a) N-SWCNTs and C-SWCNTs, and in the presence of (b) sodium azide and (c) benzoquinone.
Photogenerated OH radical kinetics measured by the degradation of pCBA in 6 mg L−1 CNT suspensions.
Summary of reactive oxygen species production under simulated sunlight exposure in 6 mg L−1 CNT suspensions at pH = 7, 25°C.
Comparison of [1O2]ss and [OH]ss of N-SWCNTs and C-SWCNTs to those reported for other nanomaterials and surface waters.

row.entry: Lake and shallow water body g

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Data corrected to flat water body values.

head.ce:title: Enhanced photoreactivity of amine-functionalized carbon nanotubes under sunlight in the aquatic environment

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Environmental Engineering, National Chung Hsing University, Taichung City 402, Taiwan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To overcome the hydrophobic nature of pristine carbonaceous materials such as carbon nanotubes (CNTs) and to make them available for intended applications, chemically covalent functionalization tailoring these materials is widely applied. However, the addition of surface functional moieties often changes the fundamental properties of the parent materials and introduces great variations that hinder a full understanding of and unified conclusions about their environmental implications. In this work, we studied the photoactivity of covalently functionalized CNTs in the aquatic environment under sunlight irradiation. The results indicate an enhanced photoreactivity of CNTs with amine functional groups resulting from a greater excited triplet state formation and a restored electronic structure after the secondary functionalization. Photogenerated singlet oxygen was produced directly through a photosensitization process in which the photoexcited CNTs transferred energy to oxygen, as well as produced indirectly from the aqueous reactions of superoxide radical. The superior photoreactive behaviors of engineered nanomaterials with amine functionalization in terms of reactive oxygen species generation in aquatic environments not only raise ecological concerns, but also render these functionalized engineered nanomaterials useful as water treatment agents against pollutants or microorganisms that can be destroyed by singlet oxygen or hydroxyl radicals.

ce:section.ce:para: The unique electronic, chemical, and mechanical features of carbonaceous materials, including fullerenes, carbon nanotubes (CNTs), and graphene, have made them leading materials in engineering applications and research (Shiu et al., 2014). Despite the exceptional physical and chemical characteristics that led to numerous practical applications and scientific developments, pristine carbon allotropes commonly suffer from their hydrophobic nature and therefore also suffer from their fatal aggregations in aqueous systems (Kawamoto et al., 2016). To avoid the inevitable aggregation and improve the processability of pristine carbonaceous materials, various modifications have been applied. Covalent functionalization that introduces several hydrophilic functional groups (e.g., hydroxyl, carbonyl, and carboxyl) through oxidative wet chemical treatments is among the most promising strategies for overcoming the substantial van der Waals interactions within pristine carbon allotropes (Balasubramanian and Burghard, 2005; Langley and Fairbrother, 2007). The oxidation steps introduce functional groups that exhibit relatively high surface energies and thus improve separation and homogeneity, which greatly facilitates the processability of carbon allotropes in subsequent solution-based systems. Moreover, oxidatively introduced functional groups, especially carboxyl groups, provide anchoring sites for the further chemical coupling of molecules (Balasubramanian and Burghard, 2005) by forming amide or ester bonds to achieve new applications that cannot otherwise be fulfilled by pristine carbon allotropes. Indeed, ever since Haddon and co-workers demonstrated that single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) with functionalization via amidation were well dispersed in the liquid phase and became versatile precursors to copolymer materials or ligands for metal complexation (Chen et al., 1998), various engineering applications using CNTs with a wide range of functional moieties have been reported (Balasubramanian and Burghard, 2005; Hirsch, 2002; Sun et al., 2002).

The incorporation of functional groups via covalent chemistry tends to persistently alter the electronic and surface properties of pristine carbon allotropes in addition to improving their dispersion capabilities. For example, the resulting products with covalent functionalization exhibit enlarged surface areas and improved adsorption capacity for volatile organic compounds (Li et al., 2016) and for heavy metal ions (Zhang et al., 2015). Liu et al. characterized the biopersistence of CNTs, indicating a much greater biodegradation of carboxylated tubes in a phagolysosomal simulant, whereas ozone-treated and aryl-sulfonated tubes showed no degradation under the same conditions (Liu et al., 2010). Moreover, the electrochemical properties and pseudocapacitive nature of CNTs were found to correlate with the oxygen-, sulfur-, and nitrogen-containing functional groups tethered to CNT surfaces (Park et al., 2013). In addition, their increasing dispersion ability could result in stable carbonaceous materials that might be transported over longer distances with enhanced overall bioavailability and possible adverse effects on human health or ecotoxicity. Although debates and concerns still remain about the toxicity of carbonaceous materials, it is generally recognized that chemical modification plays a key role in determining their behaviors and adverse impacts (Liu et al., 2013). An enhanced cytotoxicity has been observed in CNTs (Magrez et al., 2006) and graphene (Nguyen and Rodrigues, 2018) with oxidized functionalization when carbonyl (CO), carboxyl (COOH), and/or hydroxyl (OH) groups are present on their surface, and defective sites were suggested as main mediators of the acute pulmonary toxicity and genotoxicity of CNTs (Muller et al., 2008).

Amides are among the main derivatives of carboxylated CNTs (Gabriel et al., 2006). The commercially available CNTs with nitrogen-containing surface groups generally contain either amide moieties or amide linkages with a primary amine group at the end of the carbon chain (Verdugo et al., 2014). These nitrogen-functionalized CNTs have a growing number of reported applications (Wanekaya et al., 2006; Yang et al., 2007), particularly in biological applications, due to the interactions of the amino groups on CNTs with some DNA and proteins. In addition, amine-modified CNTs were reported as effective adsorbents for dye (Maleki et al., 2017) and metal removal (Zhang et al., 2015), efficient carbon dioxide adsorbents at ambient temperatures (Ye et al., 2012), and supercapacitors with notable rate capabilities (Park et al., 2013). Whereas great advances in a wide range of applications have been achieved in the past few decades, knowledge regarding the environmental behavior and/or risk assessment of carbonaceous materials and their derivatives, including amine-functionalized engineered nanomaterials, is still limited yet is highly desirable for developing a sustainable design process. Indeed, amine-functionalized CNTs were recently reported as sources and precursors for N-nitrosodimethylamine (NDMA) formation, and the chemical structure of the surface N moiety influences NDMA formation (Verdugo et al., 2014).

Photoreaction of nanomaterials released into sunlit natural waters is one of their primary fates in the aquatic environment. Photoactive nanomaterials may undergo direct photolysis via the absorption of solar radiation, whereas indirect phototransformations of nanomaterials are mediated by the chromophoric components in environmental waters. Photochemical processes, including the photoinduced production of reactive oxygen species (ROS), convert light energy into chemical energy, which mediates the transformations of nanomaterials. Photo-driven ROS production and subsequent oxidative stress may mediate toxicity to cells by decreasing the mitochondrial membrane potential. CNTs with chemical modifications exhibit distinct photoreactivity relative to their parent-forms. Aqueous colloidal dispersions of both carboxylated and PEGylated SWCNTs generate ROS, including singlet oxygen (1O2), superoxide anion (O2
−), and hydroxyl radicals (OH) in light within the solar spectrum (Chen and Jafvert, 2010). However, under similar experimental conditions, but with a surfactant added as a dispersing agent, aqueous dispersions of unfunctionalized SWCNTs exhibited no measurable ROS production (Chen and Jafvert, 2011). Moreover, except for functionality, the physicochemical properties resulting from various synthesis methods and treatments could also lead to differences in the CNTs' behaviors. A recent comparison under well-defined conditions of carboxylated CNTs from different sources suggested that high-defect-density, small-diameter, and semiconducting-rich CNTs have a greater measured excited triplet state formation and therefore singlet oxygen yield (Chen and Zepp, 2015). These higher energy CNTs may be significant in affecting the photochemical interaction/degradation of certain compounds in the aquatic environment. Derivatized SWCNTs were found to be significantly more photoreactive than derivatized multiwalled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs), and the most photoreactive CNTs exhibited comparable photoreactivity to reference natural organic matter (NOM) with the same mass-based concentration (Chen and Zepp, 2015). Nevertheless, the photoreactivity of amine-functionalized CNTs in natural environments has not yet been investigated; this is urgently needed given their wide usage.

Here, we demonstrate that the photoreactivity of carbonaceous materials can be significantly enhanced by introducing nitrogen-containing functional groups. The amine-functionalized CNTs were found to greatly promote excited triplet state generation under sunlight irradiation, and thus to greatly promote efficient ROS harvesting. In addition to energy transfer, we found an enhanced electron transfer from the triplet states of amine-functionalized CNTs to oxygen. The produced superoxide radicals were further transformed to singlet oxygen and hydroxyl radicals. The distinctive photoreactivity of CNTs tailored with specific functional groups in aqueous environments not only establishes a basis for identifying the hazards of functionalized CNTs in aquatic systems but also sets a useful guidance for the development of desired applications involving triplet states or ROS.

The chemicals were of the highest purity available from Sigma Aldrich (St. Louis, MO) and Acros Organics (Morris Plains, NJ) unless stated otherwise and were used without further purification. Aqueous solutions were prepared in water purified to ≥18.2 MΩ cm. CNTs were from NanoLab, Inc. (Waltham, MA). According to the vendor, pristine CNTs produced by chemical vapor deposition were refluxed in concentrated sulfuric/nitric acid and then reacted with ethylenediamine, resulting in the CNTs hereafter denoted C-SWCNTs and N-SWCNTs, respectively (Fig. 1 ). The N-SWCNTs has surface groups with an amide linkage and a primary amine group. Aqueous dispersions of two SWCNTs were prepared by a probe-sonication process. Briefly, 5 mg of CNTs were probe-sonicated in 50 mL of pure water in an ice-water bath under 15 W for 26 min. After probe-sonication, the CNT suspensions were centrifuged at 10,000g for 30 min, followed by collecting 70–80% of the supernatant. The basic aqueous properties and photographic illustrations of two CNT suspension are listed in Table S1. CNT suspensions were stable during the experimental duration and the stability was monitored by UV–visible absorption spectra. The CNT concentrations used in this study were approximately 6 mg L−1, determined from a pre-determined calibration curve of CNTs based on the UV–Vis absorbance. The slopes of the calibration curves at 800 nm were 0.024 mg−1 L cm−1, consistent with the reported values of SG65 SWCNT in aqueous surfactants at a wavelength of 891 nm (0.022 mg−1 L cm−1) (Hou et al., 2014).

The SWCNT thermal decomposition profiles were determined using thermogravimetry (TG) and differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) techniques (TGA; STA 6000, PerkinElmer). 2 mg of CNTs were combusted in air (20 mL/min) at a rate of 10 °C per minute to 840 °C. SWCNTs were characterized by Raman spectroscopy (Tokyo Instrument, Nanofinder 30) at a wavelength of 633 nm, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (ULVAC-PHI, PHI 5000 VersaProbe/Scanning ESCA Microprobe), and zeta-potential measurements (Malvern, UK).

Solar irradiation was conducted in an Atlas SunTest CPS+ solar simulator (Atlas Materials Testing Technology, Chicago, IL) equipped with a 1 kW xenon arc lamp with an incident irradiance of 0.065 W/cm2 (Chen and Zepp, 2015). The irradiance of the solar simulator is similar to mid-summer, midday natural sunlight. The exposure conditions were set to take into account the sunlight effects under natural environmental conditions. Throughout irradiation, the tubes were maintained at 25°C in a recirculating water bath. C-SWCNT suspensions were prepared by probe-sonication and centrifugation, resulting in approximately 6 mg L−1 calculated by the pre-determined calibration curves. In addition, in every set of experiments, N-SWCNT samples were optically matched to the C-SWCNTs to have the same light absorption within the solar irradiation range. During the experiments, the samples were periodically removed from the light source and filtered through 0.2 μm membrane filters prior to subsequent analysis.

The production and concentrations of different ROS were measured using specific scavengers. The production of 1O2 was monitored via the loss of furfuryl alcohol (FFA) through which the pseudo-steady-state concentration of 1O2 was also determined (Haag and Hoigne, 1986). The reaction between FFA and 1O2 is nearly diffusion controlled with 1:1 stoichiometry. The FFA concentration (0.2 mM) used was sufficiently low that the photochemical loss of furfuryl alcohol kept first-order kinetics, and the steady-state concentration of singlet oxygen was not significantly repressed (Haag and Hoigne, 1986; Haag and Mill, 1987). FFA was analyzed by high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) using a C18 column with acetonitrile/water (30:70) as the mobile phase. p-Chlorobenzoic acid (pCBA) was used as a hydroxyl radical (OH) scavenger. After scavenging OH, residual pCBA was determined by HPLC with the UV/Vis detector set at 230 nm. HPLC separation was on a standard C18 analytical column using acetonitrile/water (14:86) containing 40 mM of a phosphate buffer (pH = 7) as the mobile phase. Details of the methods and calculation for the steady-state concentration of singlet oxygen and OH radical are provided in Text S1 in the Supporting information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determining the formation rates of triplet states
ce:section.ce:para: The formation rates of the excited triplet state of CNTs under sunlight were probed by photo-driven isomerization of one of the dienes, sorbic acid ((2E,4E)-hexa-2,4-dienoic acid, t,t-HDA) (Grebel et al., 2011; Parker et al., 2013; Zeng and Arnold, 2012; Zepp et al., 1985). In simulated sunlight experiments, irradiance lower than 316 nm was filtered out to eliminate the direct photolysis of t,t-HDA. Four HDA isomers ((2Z,4E)-hexa-2,4-dienoic acid (c,t-HDA), (2Z,4Z)-hexa-2,4-dienoic acid (c,c-HDA), (2E,4E)-hexa-2,4-dienoic acid (t,t-HDA), and (2E,4Z)-hexa-2,4-dienoic acid (t,c-HDA)), including the reformation of t,t-HDA itself, would be formed after relaxation of the electronically excited sorbate back to its ground state (Fig. S1). The major product isomer, c,t-HDA, was measured by HPLC using a C18 column with 15% acetonitrile and an 85% acetate buffer (30 mM, pH = 4.75) detected at UV 254 nm. Details on the triplet state measurements can be found in a previous study (Chen and Zepp, 2015).

Thermal gravimetric analysis (TGA) and DSC were frequently used to identify the oxidation temperatures of the components in CNTs. Adsorbed molecules and carbonaceous fractions (e.g., amorphous carbon) generally oxidized by 600 °C, followed by the combustion of graphitic carbon at 600–700 °C, with the final remaining weight above 850 °C corresponding to the metal impurities (Dillon et al., 1999; Wepasnick et al., 2010). Sharp weight loss appeared at 630 and 680 °C for N-SWCNTs and C-SWCNTs, respectively (Fig. 2 ). Most of the organic functional moieties decomposed prior to the onset of CNT weight loss (Liu et al., 2005). The downshifted TGA pattern of the amine functionalized tubes from the oxidized CNTs is attributed to the decomposition of the nitrogen-containing surface groups, which is consistent with those reported previously (Jimeno et al., 2009; Silva et al., 2012; Wang et al., 2006).

Raman spectra of CNTs typically consist of a G-band and a D-band. It has been established that the G band peak is associated with the tangential, in-plane stretching vibration of the CC bonds within the pristine graphitic surface of CNTs. The D band peak at approximately 1300 cm−1 is induced by surface structural disorder originating from surface defect sites and adsorbed amorphous carbon (Wepasnick et al., 2010). Hence, the ratio of the intensities of the D and G bands (ID/IG) can be useful as quantitative metrics for the presence of defects, covalent sidewall derivations, and/or amorphous carbon. The ID/IG value at 633 nm laser excitation of C-SWCNTs was higher than that of the N-SWCNTs (Fig. 3 ). Given the major decomposition of C-SWCNTs observed in the TGA analysis that occurred at temperatures between 600 and 700 °C, which is associated with the graphitic carbon content, rather than at lower temperatures (approximately 400–550 °C) that belongs to carboxylated carbon fragments/amorphous carbon (Worsley et al., 2009), the higher ID/IG value of C-SWCNTs was therefore likely due to higher defect densities in the tubes. The reduction of the ID/IG ratio after the secondary functionalization, i.e., incorporation of the amine functional groups to carboxylated CNTs, was consistent with a previous study (Park et al., 2013).

Fig. 4 shows the electrophoretic mobilities (EPMs) of two CNTs, determined as a function of pH. Over the entire range of concentrations, both CNTs are negatively charged and become more negative as pH values increase due to the deprotonation of carboxylated groups. The hydrophilic nature of both functionalized CNTs is consistent with their high negative surface charge. The CNTs terminating in amine groups slightly shifted the electrophoretic mobilities to less negative values, which again supports the surface modification and is in line with a previous study (Shen et al., 2007).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Excited triplet state formation of CNTs under sunlight irradiation
ce:section.ce:para: The photoexcitation of substances generally results mainly in excited singlet states, and the singlet states find one of the three transitions: singlet-singlet emission (i.e., fluorescence), non-radiative conversion back to the ground state, or transition to excited triplet states through intersystem crossing. For CNTs, the relatively longer lifetimes of excited triplet states than those of excited singlet states suggest that quenching of these excited triplet states may play a major role in most of their photochemical interactions with other compounds (Chen and Zepp, 2015; Park et al., 2011). Quenching of the excited triplet state of CNTs through energy transfer to dienes (i.e., t,t-HDA in the present study) was thus used for probing the excited triplet state formation of CNTs. In the unirradiated samples, the presence of isomers in the solutions was negligible, except for the unisomerized t,t-HDA. Under sunlight irradiation, the photosensitization of t,t-HDA by CNTs occurs rapidly only when the triplet energies of the CNT excited states are equal to or greater than the excited state energy of t,t-HDA (Grebel et al., 2011; Sharpless and Blough, 2014; Zepp et al., 1985). Therefore, this approach is utilized for probing the excited triplet states of CNTs with energies higher than approximately 2.1 eV. Among the four HDA isomers (c,t-HDA, c,c-HDA, t,t-HDA, and t,c-HDA) formed after relaxation of the electronically excited sorbate back to its ground state, c,t-HDA was the major isomer product. The formation of c,t-HDA under sunlight irradiation was therefore used for quantification (Grebel et al., 2011). In Fig. 6 , under sunlight irradiation, a much higher c,t-HDA formation with time was induced by N-SWCNTs, which indicates an enhanced excited triplet state formation of N-SWCNTs.

It was previously demonstrated by Jin et al. that amine-functionalization can cause a decrease in the band gap of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as evidenced by the redshift of the photoluminescence emission spectra and density functional theory (Jin et al., 2013). The authors suggested that the band gap of the GQDs can be tuned by the charge transfer between the functional groups and GQDs. The unpaired electrons of the amine functional groups can donate to the antibonding state in the benzene ring, which increases the electron density and results in lowering the GQD band gap. Similar to the effect of alkyl functional groups on the graphene surface, the amine functionalization of the SWCNTs likely would cause a decrease in the band gap. Accordingly, the smaller band gap of N-SWCNTs, coupled with the reported rapid singlet-to-triplet intersystem crossing behavior of CNTs (Park et al., 2011), could be responsible for the higher triplet state yield in N-SWCNTs observed by the higher c,t-HDA formation. Indeed, a primary amine at the edge was shown to significantly change the entire electronic structure of the graphene surface, the resonance character between the delocalized π orbital and the molecule orbital in the amine groups leading to a narrower optical band gap (Tetsuka et al., 2012; Zhu et al., 2015).

A common source of 1O2 is energy transfer from an electronically excited sensitizer which absorbs the light to the oxygen (Foote et al., 1995). Alternately, 1O2 can be generated indirectly via O2
− (Brunet et al., 2009; Koppenol, 1976). Triplet-state fullerene was indicated as the primary mediator of energy or electron transfer to oxygen, leading to 1O2 or O2
− formation, respectively (Arbogast et al., 1991). Benzoquinone, a known superoxide quencher, was applied in the 1O2 measurement, to evaluate the role of O2
− in singlet oxygen formation. In the presence of benzoquinone, [1O2]ss was reduced to one third (4.2 × 10−13 M) and one half (2.4 × 10−13 M) for N-SWCNTs and C-SWCNTs, respectively. The suppressed singlet oxygen production with the benzoquinone addition to both CNTs not only indicated O2
− formation during the excited triplet CNTs relaxation process but also showed that O2
− served as one of the singlet oxygen sources. Functionalized MWCNTs were previously shown to photogenerate 1O2 and OH, but no detectable O2
−, during UVA irradiation (Qu et al., 2013), which we hypothesize may be attributed to a favorable transition of O2
− to 1O2.

A comparison of the capacitive behavior of CNTs with different covalent functional groups by Park et al. showed that although CNTs functionalized with carboxylic groups exhibited greater capacitance over the amine-functionalized CNT, the amine-functionalized CNT showed much better capacitance retention relative to pristine CNTs (Park et al., 2013). This could be attributed to the higher electrical resistance of carboxylated CNTs due to the disruption of the intrinsic conjugated structure, while the restoration of the electronic structure by secondary functionalization (amidation) leads to a favorable capacitance fading (Park et al., 2013). As supported by the lower ID/IG value in Raman spectra, the N-SWCNTs has restored the electronic structure from the disrupted conjugated framework in the C-SWCNTs. As a consequence, the enhanced excited triplet yield facilitated both 1O2 and O2
− production, while the restored electronic structure of functionalized amine also further promotes 1O2 production indirectly through O2
−.

In addition to the amine-based groups, oxygen-based groups also contribute to the surface optical states in CNTs. Although carboxylated CNTs exhibited favorable photo-driven ROS production compared to the pristine ones (Chen and Jafvert, 2011), the carboxylic and epoxide groups were suggested as non-radiative electron-hole recombination centers (Zhu et al., 2015). Comparative photoluminescence spectra of CNTs in Fig. S2 were then conducted and showed that C-SWCNTs have a negligible singlet-to-singlet emission. This may be attributed to the aforementioned non-radiative electron-hole recombination occurred on oxygen-based functional groups, and/or the possible newly formed optically allowed states by the amine functionalization (Piao et al., 2013). In the work of Yang and co-workers (Zhu et al., 2012), modification of the COOH and epoxy groups on graphene surfaces with alkylamines into CONHR and CNHR, respectively, reduced non-radiative recombination and transformed the GQD from defect-state emission to intrinsic-state emission. Carboxylated groups on the C-SWCNT surface acted as non-radiative electron-hole recombination centers, which suppressed the triplet excited states. Indeed, the lower excited triplet state formation rate observed in Fig. 6 indicated lower singlet excited states yield and/or additional deactivation channels which compete with the intersystem crossing. Additionally, N-SWCNTs was a secondary functionalization product of C-SWCNTs, therefore both would contain the same residual metallic catalysts from the synthesis process. It should be noted, however, that the significant involvement of available metals released from CNTs during energy transfer from excited triplet state to oxygen has been excluded (Chen and Zepp, 2015).

pCBA has shown negligible photolysis under UV irradiation (Li et al., 2018) and was therefore applied as an OH probe with a reaction rate constant of 5.2 × 109 M−1 s−1 (Elovitz et al., 2000). As shown in Fig. 8 , the steady-state concentrations of photogenerated OH ([OH]ss) under sunlight of N-SWCNTs and C-SWCNTs were 1.6 × 10−16 M and 5.4 × 10−17 M, respectively. The higher excited triplet formation and facilitated electron transfer attributed to the restored electronic structure explain the higher OH oxidation in N-SWCNTs.

In natural waters, natural organic matter is important mediator in generating reactive oxygen species. The photoreactivity of both CNTs used in this study was compared to other CNTs, nanomaterials, NOM, and surface waters (Table 1 ). The [1O2]ss normalized with mass concentration of amine-functionalized CNTs was higher than that of other functionalized CNTs, MWCNTs, and aqueous C60 clusters (nC60), and was comparable to [1O2]ss generated by dissolved black carbon exposed to a UVA light at a lower intensity. In comparison to natural organic matter, ROS production of N-SWCNTs was comparable or higher. Additionally, electrolytes are abundant in the natural environment. It was found in a previous study that neither the addition of electrolytes to the system nor the aggregation resulting from altering ionic strength significantly affect photo-driven energy transfer process in CNTs (Chen and Zepp, 2015). The less-closely packed feature of chemically modified CNTs likely resembles fullerol, excluding the possibility of significant reduction of energy transfer efficiency via triplet-triplet annihilation.

Given the wide variations and rapid development of emerging engineered nanomaterials, the limited understanding, and conflicting reports regarding their implications for the aquatic environment, this study aimed to provide an understanding for assessing the potential adverse effects of chemically modified CNTs. The comparative influence on the photoactivity of carboxylic and amine functional groups tethered to SWCNTs, which has not previously been explored, was investigated in this work and is summarized in Fig. 9 . Amine functionalized CNTs exhibited a greater excited triplet state yield, which promoted 1O2 and O2
− production via the energy and electron transfer routes, respectively. The O2
− was subsequently transformed to 1O2 and OH. Accordingly, 1O2 was produced directly through the photosensitization process in which the photoexcited CNTs transfer energy to oxygen, and indirectly from the aqueous reactions of O2
−, which was evaluated by the addition of an O2
− scavenger (i.e., benzoquinone) in the measurement of 1O2 production. The enhanced photochemical behavior of amine-functionalized CNTs was attributed to an efficient singlet to triplet transition and the restoration of the electronic structure throughout the secondary functionalization process. Because the production of ROS has been used as an indicator of harmful ecological risks and a transformation promotor of the parent compounds, the photoactivity of SWCNTs with different functional groups tethered to CNTs provides insights into the environmental implications of covalent modification, as well as offering guidance for the emerging functionalized engineered materials and their intended applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge financial support from the Ministry of Science and Technology (MOST) of Taiwan under grant number MOST 105-2221-E-005-003-MY3.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2018.04.241.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Comparative photoactivity and antibacterial properties of C60 fullerenes and titanium dioxide nanoparticles
Photoreactivity of carboxylated single-walled carbon nanotubes in sunlight: reactive oxygen species production in water
The role of surface functionalization in the solar light-induced production of reactive oxygen species by single-walled carbon nanotubes in water
Probing photosensitization by functionalized carbon nanotubes
Solution properties of single-walled carbon nanotubes
A simple and complete purification of single-walled carbon nanotube materials
Hydroxyl radical/ozone ratios during ozonation processes. II. The effect of temperature, ph, alkalinity, and dom properties
Photochemistry of dissolved black carbon released from biochar: reactive oxygen species generation and phototransformation
Preparation and characterisation of single-walled carbon nanotubes functionalised with amines
Sorbic acid as a quantitative probe for the formation, scavenging and steady-state concentrations of the triplet-excited state of organic compounds
Singlet oxygen in surface waters. 3. Photochemical formation and steady-state concentrations in various types of waters
Rate constants for interaction of 1O2 (1∆g) with azide ion in water
Photochemistry of aqueous C60 clusters: evidence of 1O2 formation and its role in mediating C60 phototransformation
Photoreactivity of unfunctionalized single-wall carbon nanotubes involving hydroxyl radical: chiral dependency and surface coating effect
Effects of amine molecular structure on carbon nanotubes functionalization
Tuning the photoluminescence of graphene quantum dots through the charge transfer effect of functional groups
Reactions involving singlet oxygen and the superoxide anion
Effect of wet chemical treatments on the distribution of surface oxides on carbonaceous materials
Characterization of volatile organic compound adsorption on multiwall carbon nanotubes under different levels of relative humidity using linear solvation energy relationship
Effects of chloride ions on dissolution, ROS generation, and toxicity of silver nanoparticles under UV irradiation
Chemical modification of single-walled carbon nanotubes with peroxytrifluoroacetic acid
Biodurability of single-walled carbon nanotubes depends on surface functionalization
Understanding the toxicity of carbon nanotubes
Dispersion, interfacial interaction and re-agglomeration of functionalized carbon nanotubes in epoxy composites
